section.title: The Day of Atonement
section.sectionRationale: This is the central, climactic ritual of Leviticus, given after the death of Nadab and Abihu and the purity laws of chapters 11 through 15, the once a year answer to accumulated sin and uncleanness, standing just before the Holiness Code begins in chapter 17.
section.thesis: On the Day of Atonement, once each year, the high priest enters the Most Holy Place with blood to atone for himself, the sanctuary, and all the people, while a second goat visibly bears their sins away into the wilderness, foreshadowing Christ's once for all atonement and the complete removal of sin.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the warning after Nadab and Abihu and Aaron's careful entry; the blood of the bull and the goat sprinkled in the Most Holy Place; the scapegoat sent away bearing Israel's sins; and the permanent statute establishing an annual day of atonement.

units.label: Not freely, but carefully
units.note: after the death of two sons

media.alt: A veiled entrance before the ark
media.caption: Aaron may not enter the Most Holy Place freely, only as commanded, and once a year.

verses.text: Now the LORD spoke to Moses after the death of two of Aaron’s sons when they approached the presence of the LORD.

And the LORD said to Moses: “Tell your brother Aaron not to enter freely into the {{qodesh|Most Holy Place}} behind the veil in front of the {{kapporet|mercy seat}} on the ark, or else he will die, because I appear in the cloud above the mercy seat.
This is how Aaron is to enter the Holy Place: with a young bull for a sin offering and a ram for a burnt offering.

He is to wear the sacred linen tunic, with linen undergarments. He must tie a linen sash around him and put on the linen turban. These are holy garments, and he must bathe himself with water before he wears them.
And he shall take from the congregation of Israel two male goats for a sin offering and one ram for a burnt offering.

units.label: Two goats, one lot
units.note: for the LORD, and for the scapegoat

verses.text: Aaron is to present the bull for his sin offering and make {{kipper|atonement}} for himself and his household.
Then he shall take the two goats and present them before the LORD at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting.
After Aaron casts lots for the two goats, one for the LORD and the other for the {{azazel|scapegoat}},
he shall present the goat chosen by lot for the LORD and sacrifice it as a sin offering.
But the goat chosen by lot as the scapegoat shall be presented alive before the LORD to make atonement by sending it into the wilderness as the scapegoat.

units.label: Blood behind the veil
units.note: for himself, and for the people

verses.text: When Aaron presents the bull for his sin offering and makes atonement for himself and his household, he is to slaughter the bull for his own sin offering.
Then he must take a censer full of burning coals from the altar before the LORD, and two handfuls of finely ground fragrant incense, and take them inside the veil.
He is to put the incense on the fire before the LORD, and the cloud of incense will cover the mercy seat above the Testimony, so that he will not die.
And he is to take some of the bull’s blood and sprinkle it with his finger on the east side of the mercy seat; then he shall sprinkle some of it with his finger seven times before the mercy seat.

Aaron shall then slaughter the goat for the sin offering for the people and bring its blood behind the veil, and with its blood he must do as he did with the bull’s blood: He is to sprinkle it against the mercy seat and in front of it.

So he shall make atonement for the Most Holy Place because of the impurities and rebellious acts of the Israelites in regard to all their sins. He is to do the same for the Tent of Meeting which abides among them, because it is surrounded by their impurities.

No one may be in the Tent of Meeting from the time Aaron goes in to make atonement in the Most Holy Place until he leaves, after he has made atonement for himself, his household, and the whole assembly of Israel.
Then he shall go out to the altar that is before the LORD and make atonement for it. He is to take some of the bull’s blood and some of the goat’s blood and put it on all the horns of the altar.
He is to sprinkle some of the blood on it with his finger seven times to cleanse it and consecrate it from the uncleanness of the Israelites.

media.alt: A goat led out toward the wilderness
media.caption: The live goat carries Israel's confessed sins away into a solitary place.

verses.text: When Aaron has finished purifying the Most Holy Place, the Tent of Meeting, and the altar, he is to bring forward the live goat.

Then he is to lay both hands on the head of the live goat and confess over it all the iniquities and rebellious acts of the Israelites in regard to all their sins. He is to put them on the goat’s head and send it away into the wilderness by the hand of a man appointed for the task.
The goat will carry on itself all their iniquities into a solitary place, and the man will release it into the wilderness.

units.label: Washed, and burned outside the camp

verses.text: Then Aaron is to enter the Tent of Meeting, take off the linen garments he put on before entering the Most Holy Place, and leave them there.

He is to bathe himself with water in a holy place and put on his own clothes. Then he must go out and sacrifice his burnt offering and the people’s burnt offering to make atonement for himself and for the people.
He is also to burn the fat of the sin offering on the altar.
The man who released the goat as the scapegoat must wash his clothes and bathe himself with water; afterward he may reenter the camp.

The bull for the sin offering and the goat for the sin offering, whose blood was brought into the Most Holy Place to make atonement, must be taken outside the camp; and their hides, flesh, and dung must be burned up.
The one who burns them must wash his clothes and bathe himself with water, and afterward he may reenter the camp.
This is to be a permanent statute for you: On the tenth day of the seventh month, you shall humble yourselves and not do any work, whether the native or the foreigner who resides among you,
because on this day atonement will be made for you to cleanse you, and you will be clean from all your sins before the LORD.
It is a Sabbath of complete rest for you, that you may humble yourselves; it is a permanent statute.
The priest who is anointed and ordained to succeed his father as high priest shall make atonement. He will put on the sacred linen garments
and make atonement for the Most Holy Place, the Tent of Meeting, and the altar, and for the priests and all the people of the assembly.
This is to be a permanent statute for you, to make atonement once a year for the Israelites because of all their sins.” And all this was done as the LORD had commanded Moses.

qodesh.gloss: The Most Holy Place, literally holy of holies, the innermost chamber of the tabernacle housing the ark. Only the high priest could enter, only once a year, only with blood, marking it as the point of greatest access to, and greatest danger before, God's presence.

kapporet.gloss: The mercy seat, the golden lid of the ark, from the same root as kaphar, to atone or cover. Blood sprinkled here, before the very place of God's manifest presence, made atonement possible for Israel's worst failures.

kipper.gloss: To atone, to cover, to make amends. Repeated throughout this chapter for the priest, the sanctuary, the altar, and the people, showing that sin defiles more than the sinner, and that a costly, blood based covering is required to deal with it.

azazel.gloss: A term of uncertain origin, traditionally rendered scapegoat, the goat that goes away. Whatever its precise etymology, the ritual meaning is clear: this goat visibly carries Israel's confessed sins away from the camp into a solitary place, never to return.

basic.context: Once a year, on the Day of Atonement, the high priest alone enters the Most Holy Place with the blood of a bull and a goat to atone for himself and the people. A second goat has Israel's sins confessed over it and is sent away into the wilderness, visibly carrying their guilt out of the camp.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real, once a year ritual for Israel's old covenant priesthood, not a pattern Christians repeat. Its significance is what it reveals: sin defiles the sinner, the sanctuary, and the whole community, and only costly, blood based atonement, applied at the point of God's own presence, can deal with it. Hebrews reads the whole ritual as pointing forward to Christ.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on kipper, to atone or cover, applied to the priest, the Most Holy Place, the altar, and the people in turn. The kapporet, mercy seat, gets its name from this same root: it is literally the place of atoning. The second goat, associated with azazel, does not receive blood but confession, and carries sin away bodily.
basic.typology: The two goats together picture one complete work: the first goat's blood satisfies what sin requires at the place of God's presence, and the second goat visibly removes what has been atoned for. Hebrews 9 to 10 reads this whole day as a rehearsal awaiting Christ, who is both the sacrifice whose blood is brought into the true Most Holy Place and the one who bears away sin, accomplished once for all rather than yearly.
basic.christ: Hebrews 9:11-12 says Christ entered the greater tabernacle, not with the blood of goats and calves but with His own blood, having obtained eternal redemption, once for all. He is the reality both goats pictured: His blood atones, and He bears away sin, so that, unlike Aaron, He never needs to repeat the sacrifice.
basic.god: God is so holy that even His own high priest cannot approach carelessly, on pain of death; yet God Himself provides the exact means, blood and confession, by which sinful people are cleansed and He continues to dwell among them. His holiness and His mercy meet in the same ritual.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 9:1-12 draws directly on this chapter to describe Christ's greater and more perfect priestly work. Hebrews 10:1-4 says these sacrifices could never take away sins but only foreshadowed a better one. Psalm 103:12 anticipates the scapegoat's picture: as far as the east is from the west, so far has He removed our transgressions.
basic.oneStory: The Day of Atonement gathers the whole Levitical system into one climactic day: blood applied at the place of God's presence, and sin carried bodily away. Both halves of that picture, atoning blood and the removal of guilt, meet completely in Christ, our great High Priest, who offered Himself once and bore our sins in His own body to the tree.

context.literary: This chapter is the structural and theological center of Leviticus, coming after Nadab and Abihu's death for careless worship (chapter 10) and the purity laws (chapters 11 through 15), and just before the Holiness Code begins in chapter 17; it answers both the danger of approaching God and the accumulated uncleanness of the camp.
context.historical: For Israel, this was the one day each year the high priest entered the innermost chamber of the tabernacle, an event of enormous solemnity given the death of Aaron's own sons for unauthorized worship, underscoring both the necessity and the careful regulation of atonement.
context.canonical: This ritual becomes the primary Old Testament lens through which the letter to the Hebrews interprets the work of Christ, and its vocabulary, atonement, blood, mercy seat, and the bearing away of sin, runs through the rest of Scripture's account of salvation.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish the one annual ritual capable of atoning for the sins of the whole nation and cleansing the sanctuary itself, showing both the seriousness of accumulated sin and the sufficiency of the remedy God provided.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage prescribes a specific, repeatable ritual for Israel's old covenant priesthood; Christians do not observe a Day of Atonement, because Hebrews teaches that Christ's single sacrifice has accomplished what this day could only foreshadow and repeat. Its abiding truth is that sin requires atonement, and that God Himself supplies it.

debates.question: What does the scapegoat, sent to azazel, represent?

views.label: A vivid picture of sin's removal
views.case: Most interpreters see the live goat simply as a dramatic visual aid: since blood cannot be carried away and watched leaving, a second goat physically enacts the truth that atoned sin is also removed, never to return, matching Psalm 103:12.

views.label: A reference to a wilderness demon or place
views.case: Some older and more recent scholars argue azazel names a wilderness demon or desolate place to which the goat, bearing sin, is sent back, symbolically returning evil to its own domain rather than to God's dwelling.

debates.bearing: Either reading affirms that the goat carries Israel's confessed sin decisively away from the camp and from God's presence; the debate concerns the term's precise background, not the ritual's meaning.

study.originalLanguages: Kipper, to atone or cover, is repeated for the priest, the Most Holy Place, the Tent of Meeting, the altar, and the people, showing atonement's comprehensive reach: sin stains not only the sinner but the whole community and even the sanctuary itself. The kapporet, mercy seat, takes its name from this same root, literally the covering or atoning place. The second goat, linked to the obscure term azazel, receives no blood but a confession of sin, and is sent bodily away, dramatizing what the first goat's blood accomplished.

typology.type: The goat whose blood is brought into the Most Holy Place
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who entered the true Most Holy Place with His own blood, obtaining eternal redemption once for all, Hebrews 9:11-12.

typology.type: The scapegoat sent into the wilderness bearing Israel's sins
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who bore our sins in His own body on the tree, removing them as far as the east is from the west, 1 Peter 2:24, Psalm 103:12.

typology.type: The annual repetition of the ritual
typology.fulfillment: Superseded by Christ's single, unrepeatable offering, since a sacrifice repeated yearly could never perfect those who draw near, Hebrews 10:1-4, 10.

study.christ: Hebrews interprets this entire chapter as a shadow of Christ's priestly work. Where Aaron entered a man made Most Holy Place yearly with the blood of animals, Christ entered heaven itself once, with His own blood, securing eternal redemption, Hebrews 9:11-12, 24-26. Where the sin offering's blood covered Israel's guilt before the mercy seat, Christ's blood cleanses the conscience itself, Hebrews 9:14. And where the scapegoat carried Israel's confessed sins into the wilderness, Christ bore our sins in His own body on the tree, 1 Peter 2:24, so completely that God remembers them no more.
study.god: God's holiness is fiercely guarded: the chapter opens with the reminder that Aaron's own sons died for careless worship, and even the high priest may enter the Most Holy Place only once a year, by a strict procedure, on pain of death. Yet the entire chapter exists because God provides the remedy Himself, blood, incense, and a goat to bear sin away, so that a sinful people can be cleansed and He can continue to dwell among them without consuming them.

crossReferences.note: Christ entered the greater, heavenly sanctuary once for all with His own blood, in contrast to the yearly entry of the high priest with the blood of goats and calves.
The law's sacrifices, offered repeatedly, could never take away sins; believers are sanctified through the single offering of Jesus Christ's body.
Christ Himself bore our sins in His body on the tree, echoing the scapegoat's bearing away of Israel's iniquities.
As far as the east is from the west, so far has He removed our transgressions from us.

study.oneStory: The Day of Atonement gathers Leviticus's whole sacrificial system into one climactic day: blood applied at the very place of God's presence, and sin carried bodily away from the camp. Both halves of that picture, atonement and removal, are fulfilled completely in Christ, the great High Priest who offered Himself once and bore our sins in His own body, so that what Aaron could only repeat yearly, Jesus has finished forever.

quiz.q: Why does the chapter open by recalling the death of Aaron's two sons?

options.text: To warn that Aaron himself must not enter the Most Holy Place freely, or he too will die.
options.note: Verses 1-2 draw this connection explicitly.

options.text: To explain who would replace them as priests.
options.note: The text's stated concern is the manner of entering God's presence, not succession.

options.text: It is unrelated background information.
options.note: Verse 2 directly ties the warning to what happened to Nadab and Abihu.

options.text: To celebrate their memory.
options.note: The passage uses their death as a solemn warning, not a celebration.

quiz.q: What happened to the two goats chosen by lot?

options.text: One was sacrificed as a sin offering; the other was sent alive into the wilderness bearing the people's sins.
options.note: Verses 8-10 and 15, 20-22 describe this two part ritual.

options.text: Both were sacrificed on the altar together.
options.note: Only one goat was slaughtered; the other was released alive.

options.text: Both were released into the wilderness.
options.note: Only the scapegoat was released; the other goat was sacrificed as a sin offering.

options.text: The text does not distinguish between the two goats.
options.note: Verses 8-10 explicitly assign each goat a distinct role by lot.

quiz.q: What did Aaron do with the blood of the bull and the goat behind the veil?

options.text: He sprinkled it on and before the mercy seat to make atonement for himself, his household, and the people.
options.note: Verses 14-16 describe this sprinkling in detail.

options.text: He poured it out entirely at the base of the altar outside the tent.
options.note: The blood was sprinkled specifically before the mercy seat inside the veil, not merely poured outside.

options.text: He drank a portion of it as part of the ritual.
options.note: Leviticus elsewhere forbids consuming blood; it is applied to the mercy seat and altar, never consumed.

options.text: No blood was brought behind the veil.
options.note: Verses 14-15 explicitly describe blood brought and sprinkled behind the veil.

quiz.q: What did Aaron do over the head of the live goat before it was released?

options.text: He laid both hands on it and confessed all the iniquities of the Israelites over it.
options.note: Verse 21 describes this confession and laying on of hands.

options.text: He slaughtered it as a burnt offering.
options.note: The live goat was not slaughtered; it was released alive into the wilderness.

options.text: He simply released it without any ceremony.
options.note: Verse 21 specifically describes the laying on of hands and confession before release.

options.text: Nothing is recorded about this goat.
options.note: Verses 20-22 describe this goat's role in detail.

quiz.q: According to Hebrews, how does Christ's priestly work compare to what happens in this chapter?

options.text: Christ entered the true, heavenly sanctuary once for all with His own blood, obtaining eternal redemption, unlike the yearly entry of Israel's high priest.
options.note: Hebrews 9:11-12, 24-26 makes this contrast directly.

options.text: Christ repeats His sacrifice every year just as Aaron did.
options.note: Hebrews 10:10 stresses the opposite: Christ's offering was made once for all, not repeated.

options.text: Hebrews never connects Christ's work to this chapter.
options.note: Hebrews 9 draws extensively and directly on the Day of Atonement to describe Christ's priesthood.

options.text: Hebrews treats this chapter as having no ongoing significance.
options.note: Hebrews treats this chapter as central to understanding Christ's atoning work.

questions: Aaron could not enter God's presence carelessly, only by the exact way God prescribed. Where are you tempted to approach God casually, on your own terms, rather than through the way He has provided in Christ?

The first goat's blood satisfied what sin required; the second goat visibly carried sin away. Which of these truths, that your sin is atoned for, or that it is genuinely removed, do you find harder to believe about yourself right now?
Aaron had to make atonement for himself before he could make it for the people. Where do you need to acknowledge your own need before you are quick to address the sins of others?

This whole elaborate, costly ritual had to be repeated every single year, and still could not perfect anyone's conscience. How does knowing that Christ's one sacrifice is enough change the way you carry guilt this week?

The scapegoat carried Israel's confessed sins into a solitary place, never to return. Is there a sin or a failure you keep dragging back into the camp of your own mind, even though Christ has already carried it away?

translationNotes.note: Mercy seat translates kapporet, the golden lid of the ark, sharing its root with kaphar, to atone; some render it simply as the atonement cover, making the wordplay with the whole chapter's theme explicit.

Scapegoat translates the Hebrew azazel, a term of debated origin; whether it names a place, a being, or simply describes the goat's function, the ritual sense is unaffected: this goat visibly bears sin away from the camp.

Humble yourselves is the law's language for what later Judaism calls fasting on Yom Kippur; the text itself does not use the specific word for fasting, but the demand for self affliction was understood that way from an early date.
